coredata.dc:title: A green path to extract carbon quantum dots by coconut water: Another fluorescent probe towards Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Preethi, Manoharan
Viswanathan, Chinnuswamy
Ponpandian, Nagamony
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been of great enthrallment in the fluorescent probe field. Carbon precursors with interesting physicochemical properties may be derived from natural sources. In this research, for the first time, coconut water has been used as a source of carbon to prepare blue fluorescent CQDs without any modification and functionalization. The preparation of CQDs is very simple and cost-effective, when compared with other conventional techniques. The reaction parameters temperature and pH were varied to obtain monodispersed spherical CQDs with an average grain size of ∼5nm. The CQDs exhibit blue emission with a wavelength of 487nm with an excitation wavelength of 390nm and are used for Fe3+ ions detection. Fe3+ ions have been observed to quench the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs than other heavy metals. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescent CQDs are quenched due to the interaction of CQDs and Fe3+ ions. A spectroscopic result shows that Fe3+ ions can be observed within a concentration of 0–700μM, as well as the detection limit is 0.30μM. The CQDs provide a unique pathway for potential application in the environmental monitoring of heavy metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green preparation of CQDs
Coconut water
Stirrer-assisted technique
Detection of metal ions
Quenching efficiency
Nonfluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metal ions with CQD
Sensitivity and selectivity of Fe3+ ions
Interaction of CQDs with Fe3+ ions

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the DST-FIST, DST-PURSE, UGC-SAP, Government of India for the financial assistance to setting up the sophisticated instrumental facilities.

item-info.ce:copyright: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of monodispersed CQDs. (b) SAED pattern of CQDs. (c) The particle size distribution of CQDs plotted from ImageJ software. (d) Zeta-potential of CQDs.

(a) XRD pattern of CQD reveals the two broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ =11.91° (0.63nm) and 22.7° (0.34nm). (b) FTIR spectra of CQD show that oxygen functional groups are located at the CQDs surface. (c) Raman spectra of CQD observed in 1490cm−1 (D band) and 1690cm−1 (G band). An intensity ratio (I D/I G) of CQD is 0.87 and suggests structural defects due to disorder carbon associated with amorphous carbon. (d) UV–vis absorption peak around 266nm is found to be a transition of π–π* of carbonyl or hydroxyl group present in the coconut water and a weak absorption peak is observed with blue luminescence.

(a) PL emission spectra of CQD of various wavelengths from 340nm to 390nm. (b) The graph was drawn between the intensity of the fluorescence and the wavelength with various pH factors (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), and pH=7 has the maximum PL intensity compared to other pH conditions. (c) Temperature-dependent PL spectra between fluorescence intensity and wavelength rising from 30 to 70°C. (d) Emission wavelength vs temperature indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs has been stable at different temperatures. (e) The stability of CQDs remains constant for 3 months, suggesting that no visible agglomeration and no significant reduction in the PL intensity.
Fluorescence photographs of CQDs with the various metal ions of a concentration of about 700μM were taken under an ultraviolet light (365nm).
The Interference study for CQD within the existence of varying metal ions at a concentration of about 700μM.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum for the CQDs, which indicate varying concentrations for Fe3+ ions from 0 to 700μM. (b) A Calibration graph explains the correlation among the various relative strength and the Fe3+ concentration with a perfect correlation coefficient at optimum conditions.
Due to coordination interaction, the formation of CQD with Fe3+ ions and the electron on the excited state of CQD has been transferred to the five electrons present in the d orbital of Fe3+ ions.
Comparative analysis of CQDs made from various natural sources with the application.
Comparison of the performance of the proposed method with some existing traditional detection methods without using CQDs.

entry.cross-ref: To, Nordstrom, Cunningham, Ball, and McCleskey (1999)

head.ce:title: A green path to extract carbon quantum dots by coconut water: Another fluorescent probe towards Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been of great enthrallment in the fluorescent probe field. Carbon precursors with interesting physicochemical properties may be derived from natural sources. In this research, for the first time, coconut water has been used as a source of carbon to prepare blue fluorescent CQDs without any modification and functionalization. The preparation of CQDs is very simple and cost-effective, when compared with other conventional techniques. The reaction parameters temperature and pH were varied to obtain monodispersed spherical CQDs with an average grain size of ∼5nm. The CQDs exhibit blue emission with a wavelength of 487nm with an excitation wavelength of 390nm and are used for Fe3+ ions detection. Fe3+ ions have been observed to quench the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs than other heavy metals. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescent CQDs are quenched due to the interaction of CQDs and Fe3+ ions. A spectroscopic result shows that Fe3+ ions can be observed within a concentration of 0–700μM, as well as the detection limit is 0.30μM. The CQDs provide a unique pathway for potential application in the environmental monitoring of heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: The evolving situation of high industrialization has created a disturbing problem of the dangers of heavy metal ions presence, which has influenced both human health and the ecosystem. Iron is the foremost and second essential nutrient element in the human body, mainly present as ferric (Fe3+) and ferrous ions (Fe2+) (Zhang et al., 2014). Fe3+ ions are notable metal ions that play a significant role in the environment as well as biological systems. Fe3+ ions are mostly generated inside the liver, spleen, medulla ossium, and change into ferritin (Andrews, 1999). Explicitly, it is involved in several biological processes varying from oxygen metabolism to DNA imprinting, RNA transcription, and protein synthesis, and greasing hemoglobin's oxygen transportation ability.

The presence of high concentrated Fe3+ ions inaugurates diseases like cancer, renal failure, anemia and deteriorate the functions of the heart, pancreas, and lungs (Guo et al., 2015). Due to these adverse effects, the rapid detection of Fe3+ ions is very important. It is essential to develop technologies of quick detection of heavy metal ions including electrochemical sensors, atomic absorption spectroscopy, plasma mass spectroscopy, etc. (Liu, Ren, Meng, Fang, & Tang, 2013). Fluorescent probes have an extreme interest due to their unique properties such as rapid analysis, high sensitivity, high specificity. However, they have some disadvantages like high toxicity, easy oxidation, high cost and low photoresponse that screen their large scale preparation (Zhang et al., 2014).

On the other hand, the researchers consider an environmentally sustainable way to achieve the quantum dots by using alternate natural and green materials as precursors such as apple juice (Mehta, Jha, Basu, Singhal, & Kailasa, 2015), bagasse (Du et al., 2014), bamboo leaves (Liu, Zhao, & Zhang, 2014), cabbage (Alam, Park, Ghouri, Park, & Kim, 2015), eggshell (Wang et al., 2012a), Jinhua bergamot (Yu et al., 2015), lemon juice (He et al., 2018), mannose (Weng et al., 2015), neem gum (Phadke et al., 2015), orange juice (Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012), coriander leaves (Sachdev & Gopinath, 2015), radish (Liu et al., 2017a), ginger (Li et al., 2014), grape juice (Huang et al., 2014), banana juice (De & Karak, 2013), etc., Many methods for preparing the CQDs have already been designed, but the stirrer assisted technique has continued to gain interest due to its inexpensive, simple, eco-friendly, and convenient equipment.

The fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) occupy a notable position as a new group of nanomaterials in zero dimension carbon families. Most CQDs are achieved by different methods including arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004), laser ablation (Li et al., 2010), ultrasonic synthesis (Li et al., 2011), chemical oxidation (Hu et al., 2013), electrochemical method (Liu, Xu, Niu, Gooding, & Liu, 2016), microwave synthesis (Wang et al., 2012a), hydrothermal method (Mehta, Jha, & Kailasa, 2014), thermal decomposition (Martindale, Hutton, Caputo, & Reisner, 2015), etc. All these mentioned synthesis methods have some disadvantages including more expensive experimental setup, the requirement of high temperature and expensive chemicals (Jinendra et al., 2019a; Jinendra et al., 2019b; Srinivas et al., 2019). To overcome these disadvantages, researchers found a simple preparation technique, called stirrer assisted technique using natural resources as a carbon source for preparing the CQDs.

In our report, we propose a green synthetic approach as a cost-effective, non-toxic, short reaction time, surface passivating agent free and environmentally friendly method to develop the CQDs from coconut water with neutral pH, using stirrer assisted technique at a lower temperature of 70°C. The CQDs were characterized in detail. Also, CQDs have been investigated for their application in different metal ion detections and compared. Particularly, the CQDs have been used as a fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of the Fe3+ ions. The synthesized CQDs showed high selectivity against Fe3+ ions, which is an important analyte. Owing to the presence of Fe3+ ions arising from the non-fluorescent combination between CQDs and Fe3+ ions, the fluorescent CQDs were quenched. In the detection of Fe3+ ions, we used CQDs from coconut water via stirrer assisted technique for the use of a fluorescence-based Fe3+ sensor. This sensor is non-toxic, cheap, and is made from coconut water that is easily available and is also ideal for large scale process. Besides, this sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity at a high concentration of Fe3+ ions.

Coconut was brought from a local supermarket (Coimbatore, Tamilnadu, India). Analytical grade Magnesium chloride (MnCl2·6H2O, 99%), Calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O, 98%), Zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98%), Ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), Aluminium chloride (AlCl3, 99%), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%), Cobalt chloride (CoCl2·6H2O, 99%), Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35%), Lead chloride (PbCl2, 99%), Ruthenium chloride (RuCl3·3H2O, 99%), Mercuric chloride (HgCl2, 99.5%), Ferrous chloride (FeCl2, 98%), Copper chloride (CuCl2, 98%) and Manganese chloride (MgCl2·6H2O,99%) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich and Himedia chemicals and used without purification. Deionized water (DI) was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

X-ray diffraction pattern has been used to determine the crystal structure and grain size of the CQDs using an X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku Smart Lab) system with CuKα radiation (1.5406Å) at room temperature. The HRTEM (JEOL JEM 2100) was used to measure the particle size as well as the morphology of the CQDs. The Raman spectrum was excited by the 514nm helium-neon laser line (Horiba Jobin Yvon Lab Ram-HR). The Zeta potential was measured by using zeta analyser (Malvern). The FTIR spectra were measured using a Bruker Tensor FT-IR spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was measured by Fluoromax-4, a spectrofluorometer.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were prepared through the stirrer-assisted route and its schematic representation was described in Fig. 1 . Initially, 30mL of coconut water was heated at 70°C for 5h. The pH of coconut water was 5.2 and to enhance the PL characteristics the pH of the solution was made to 7 by adding NaOH. During heating, the sugar molecules were dehydrated, polymerized, and carbonated into nuclei, then composed of amorphous CQDs (Sevilla & Fuertes, 2009) (Hu et al., 2010). After 5h, the coconut water solution converts from transparent to light brown colour due to high oxidation and complex reactions. The precipitate was extracted using Whatmann filter paper from coconut water. Then, the obtained solution was sonicated and centrifuged for 10min at 5000rpm to extract the larger particles. The resultant solution was purified using the Whatman filter paper with a pore size of 0.2μm and dried to obtain CQDs.

The PL quantum yield (PLQY) was analyzed by evaluating the CQDs absorbance and PL intensities, utilizing quinine sulfate as a reference. The equation of PLQY includes the following:
(1)
Q
Y S
= Q
Y
S T
*
I S
/
I
S T
*
η S 2
/
η
S T
2
*
A
S T
/
A S
where S and ST indicate the CQDs and reference (quinine sulfate), A refers to the absorbance value of CQDs and reference, I represents the PL intensities of CQDs and reference and η provides the refractive index of the CQDs and reference (both are 1.33). The reference (quinine sulfate) was used as a standard for CQDs in 0.1M H2SO4 with a 54% QYST (Suzuki et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions with CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs dispersion was used to analyze the sensitivity of different inorganic metal ions, such as Mn3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ru3+, Mg2+, Co2+, Zn2+, respectively. In detail, 2mL of metal salt in DI water (700μM) was combined with 2mL of CQDs solution (0.04mg/mL) and sonicated for 5min at room temperature. Then, the PL spectra were recorded and the exciting wavelength was 390nm.

The pH of the resulting CQDs solution (0.02mg/mL, 2mL) changed from acidic (3 and 5) to basic (9 and 11) by using the 1M hydrochloric acid and 1M sodium hydroxide, this mixture was sonicated for 5minutes and recorded the PL spectra.

Static quenching and dynamic quenching are the two fundamental types of fluorescence quenching. Formula (2) and (3) show the Stern-Volmer equation where F 0 and F give the before and after PL fluorescent intensity of CQDs. The τ and τ 0 show the presence and absence of quencher in the CQDs solution. K q is the dynamic quenching rate constant and Q is the concentration of quencher in solution. Static quenching is characterized by the existence of a complex between the quencher and the ground state of the fluorophore. In Eq. (2) K SV represents the association constant. Moreover, the lifetime of the fluorophore isn’t influenced by static quenching and the lifetime measurements are the only way to differentiate between static and dynamic quenching.
(2)
F 0
F
=
τ 0
τ
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]

The morphological information of CQDs were analyzed with the help of TEM. The HR-TEM image of the CQDs showed the presence of a monodispersed spherical shape as shown in Fig. 2(a). The Amorphous nature of CQDs were analyzed by HRTEM characterization using SAED pattern (Fig. 2(b)). The particle size distribution of CQDs is plotted from ImageJ software in Fig. 2(c). The average size of prepared CQDs of around ∼5nm. These results show that the CQDs are a combination of amorphous carbon lattices. The zeta-potential of CQDs were observed at a sharp peak of −16.6mV (Fig. 2(d)). The negative potential indicates the presence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of CQDs. It is necessary for the surface of CQDs to have negative charge moieties which makes them highly soluble in water.

The XRD pattern provides the structural details of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 3(a). The diffraction peak of CQDs were identical to the standard peak diffraction (2θ =10.6°, (001) plane), with a maximum of 11.91° (d 001 =0.63nm), owing to an improvement of the sp3 spacing between the carbon atoms. Moreover, the second diffraction peak of the CQDs at about 22.7° was lower and comparable with graphite oxide ((002) planes, 2θ =26.5°).

However, the lower shift (from 26.5 degrees to 22.7 degrees) was due to enhanced carbon disorder and an increase of the sp2 (C–C) layer spacing during the carbonization process relative to the unordered crystalline form of graphite. These two lattice planes are more prominent than that of graphitic carbon structure (0.33nm) directly related to highly disordered carbon atoms and the appearance of oxygen groups could enhance the interlayer distance (Peng et al., 2012). The values are comparable with the previous reports that employed different methods to produce CQDs (Dong et al., 2012; He et al., 2018; Zhu, Tang, Zhang, & Yang, 2012).

The Fourier-Transform Infrared spectrum was generally used to assess the functional group of CQDs, as can be seen in Fig. 3(b). An absorption peak around 3460cm−1 was observed which denotes the existence of residual hydroxyl functional groups in the carbon source of coconut water (Donawade, Raghu, & Gadaginamath, 2006; Donawade, Raghu, Muddapur, & Gadaginamath, 2005; Mehta, Jha, Basu, Singhal, & Kailasa, 2015). The C–H stretching bands of the CQDs about 2940 and 2809cm−1 primarily derive from the methylene groups in the hydrocarbon chains of coconut water. Also, the peak at 1714cm−1 represents the carboxyl group of C=O (Mitra et al., 2012). The vibrational modes at 1653cm−1 and 1469cm−1 identified the carboxylate group. The strong peaks of 1160cm−1 and 982cm−1 indicated an epoxy group for C–O and also a symmetric stretching mode (Huang et al., 2013). The characteristic peak at 900–600cm−1 reveals the C–H group indicating the oxygenation process (He et al., 2018). Therefore, the FTIR results indicate that the surface of CQD with hydrophilic groups which makes them highly soluble in water.

A Raman spectrum provides information about the CQDs crystalline and vibrational modes, which is shown in Fig. 3(c). The Raman spectrum of CQDs have two characteristic peaks located at 1490cm−1 (D band), and 1690cm−1 (G band), respectively. In a 2D hexagonal graphite lattice, a D band (disorder) is ascribed from their vibration in carbon atoms with a dangling bond of disorder carbon, whereas its G band (crystalline) is connected with its sp2 bonded carbon atoms. Therefore, its intensity ratio for the D band (I D) to G band (I G) was used for determining the quality of CQDs. An intensity ratio of CQDs (I D/I G) are 0.87 that shows that structural defects due to carbon disorder associated with the amorphous phase. The Raman data revealed that the CQDs obtained from coconut water are mainly made up of sp2 graphitic carbon with sp3 carbon defects and oxygen functional groups, respectively (Wang, Wang, & Chen, 2012b).

A UV–vis absorption spectrum of prepared CQDs appears in the Fig. 3(d). An absorption peak around 266nm is found to be a transition of π–π* of carbonyl or hydroxyl group present in the coconut water (Prasannan & Imae, 2013). However, the weak absorption peak at 426nm establishes the variation in the absorption intensities concurs well with the distinction in the PL spectrum of CQD (Sun, Li, Wu, Wei, & Zeng, 2018).

The PL spectrum reveals the emission wavelength of CQDs reaches a high intensity of 487nm while using the excitation wavelength around 390nm, which is shown in the Fig. 4(a). The PL quantum yield (PLQY) for CQDs is also calculated by measuring the absorbance intensity and PL intensity of CQDs, utilizing quinine sulfate as a kind of perspective. The reference solution was prepared at 0.1M H2SO4 with a QYST of 54%. The QY of CQDs has 14.34% at a wavelength of 390nm. The product yield (PY) of CQDs can be determined as PY=(m/M)×100%, whereas the mass of a CQD is m and the mass of fresh coconut water is M. The product yield of CQDs prepared in this analysis (30mL) is approximately 25.76%.

The PL intensity of CQDs was observed in different pH (3, 5, 7, 9, 11) that was shown in the Fig. 4(b). Under an acidic and basic solutions, the decrease in PL strength is attributed towards the incorporation with H+ and OH− ions that transform the behavior of the addition or removal of a proton into the presence of functional groups on the CQD surface. We experimented with CQD under various pH conditions on the intensity of the photoluminescence. The predominant PL was found at 7.0 which is ideal when differentiating from other pH conditions for the identification of Fe3+ ions.

The fluorescence intensity of CQDs increases with increases in temperature rising from 30 to 70°C in Fig. 4(c). This was observed in the corresponding PL investigations. The fluorescence intensity increases with increased temperature, but the emission wavelength does not differ with temperature in the Fig. 4(d). This shows that CQDs exhibit a constant fluorescence intensity at different temperatures. According to the analysis, CQDs are applied for different applications under various environments (Chen, Liu, Li, Xu, & Qiao, 2017). The stability of CQDs dispersion remains constant over three months, suggesting that no precipitation has formed and there is no significant colour change in the solution. There was no visible agglomeration and no significant reduction in the PL intensity observed by PL spectra in Fig. 4(e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of Fe3+ ions

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 demonstrated the different metal ions with CQDs which can be visualized by the naked eye, including Mn3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ru3+, Mg2+, Co2+ and Zn2+ with the concentration of 700μM to determine the sensitivity and selectivity of the CQDs under UV-light (365nm). These photographs show that the great selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ ions. The Fe3+ ions diminished the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs as compared with other metal ions. This analysis showed a strong affinity between CQDs and Fe3+ ions.

Numerous PL fluorescence sensors are available in the recent decades for the extremely sensitive and selective identification of metal ions based on oxygen functional groups in the surface of CQDs which can bind to metal ions that produce complexes. The selectivity of a prepared CQDs dispersion were tested through experimenting with metal ions (including Mn3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ru3+, Co2+, Zn2+) which act as chelators with the concentration of 0–700μM.

The plot was obtained from equations that were based on the correlation between the quenching ratio of (F/F 0) in the existence of varying metal ions, while F represents the existence of metal ions with a concentration of about 700μM and F 0 be the absence of metal ions with PL fluorescence intensity, respectively. In interference studies, Fe3+ ions (700μM) and also the combinations with Fe3+ ions and other metal ions (700μM) were added to the CQDs aqueous solution, and the quenching influence of Fe3+ and the combinations of Fe3+ and Mn+ on CQDs were analyzed. The findings revealed that the coexistence of all other metal ions has minimal influence on the quenching of Fe3+ ions with CQDs and can be ignored. This finding indicates that the CQDs sensor was not responsive to other metals but responsive to Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution. By examining the graph, we found that Fe3+ ions showed its minimal value of (F/F 0). It has been hypothesized that the phenomenon of interaction with Fe3+ ions and carboxylic groups or hydroxyl groups on a surface edge of CQDs is a key cause for the detection of Fe3+ ions (Liu et al., 2017a) (Fig. 6 ).

To probe the efficiency of the proposed investigation, the PL study was undertaken to determine the Fe3+ ions of CQDs at room temperature. The CQDs samples were obtained by adding Fe3+ ions at different concentrations originating from 0 to 700μM. The PL fluorescence intensity was measured at 390nm. The observations demonstrated that “Turn-off fluorescence” is exhibited throughout the presence of Fe3+ ions which can be seen in the Fig. 7(a). The PL intensity decreases with increasing the concentration of Fe3+ ions. Our proposed method can be easily implemented, rapidly conducted to sensitively, and selectively detect Fe3+ ions. As this study reveals, this PL fluorescence technique could be adapted to detect Fe3+ ions.

The plot shows a linear correlation among the relative intensity [(F 0 − F)/F] with different concentrations treated with CQDs against fluorescence intensity. There has been a perfect linear correlation between both the Fe3+ ions concentrations and the quenching efficiency within optimal conditions. The detection limit for Fe3+ ions was determined to be 0.30μM. Accordingly, this was additionally confirmed by a Stern-Volmer plot with an ideal correlation coefficient of 0.9922 with the variation of Fe3+ ions concentrations in Fig. 7(b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of CQDs with Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The Schematic representation of CQDs with Fe3+ ions is shown in Fig. 8 . Several researchers has reported the mechanism for fluorescence quenching by adding Fe3+ ions (Liu et al., 2017b). There are enormous quantities of various oxygen-containing functional groups, especially hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups, covering the surfaces of prepared CQDs. In the case of the as-synthesized CQDs with Fe3+ ions, the large fluorescence intensity decreased mainly through two mechanisms namely either through chelating Fe3+ ions with CQDs surfaces or by the conversion of energy between surfaces of the CQDs and d orbital of Fe3+ ions. Owing to the strong interaction between CQDs and Fe3+ ions, the PL strength of the CQDs decreases with increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ions. These CQDs surface energy or electron transfer may be attributed to the ablity of Fe3+ ions to quench the fluorescence. The valence shell of the Fe3+ ions also has a 3d5 configuration, and these orbitals have also been half-filled. An electron on the excited state of CQDs is transferred to the five electrons present in the d orbital of Fe3+ ions because of co-ordination interaction. This complex formation allows the electrons to pass from the excited state of CQDs to the d orbital of Fe3+ ions and shows a quenched phenomenon.

In overview, a green synthesis approach is demonstrated and the fluorescent CQDs were prepared by a stirrer-assisted synthesis technique using coconut water as a source of carbon. The prepared CQDs showed high solubility, strong fluorescence intensity, and excitation-dependent PL emission behavior without any surface modifications, as well as being characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy that can emit blue color fluorescence under UV irradiation. A medium for detecting the Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution with a limit of detection of 0.30μM is demonstrated by the fluorescence quenching effect of the CQD with the addition of Fe3+ ion. Also, the synthesized CQDs will be a successful candidate for a new form of fluorescence and biomedical imaging, and so on. We assume these relevant and important observations can enable us to gain a detailed understanding of CQDs and explore more possible potential applications of the newly carbon-based nanostructured materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the DST-FIST, DST-PURSE, UGC-SAP, Government of India for the financial assistance to setting up the sophisticated instrumental facilities.
